quotes.text: The Stealing of the Daughters of the Lamanites The similarities between the stories in Mosiah and Judges are complex and carefully stated: Then they said, Behold, there is a feast of the Lord in Shiloh yearly in a place which is on the north side of Bethel, on the east side of the highway that goeth up from Bethel to Shechem, and on the south of Lebonah. Therefore they commanded the children of Benjamin, saying, Go and lie in wait in the vineyards; and see, and behold, if the daughters of Shiloh come out to dance in dances, then come ye out of the vineyards, and catch you every man his wife of the daughters of Shiloh, and go to the land of Benjamin (Judges 21:19-21). Now there was a place in Shemlon where the daughters of the Lamanites did gather themselves together to sing, and to dance, and to make themselves merry. And it came to pass that there was one day a small number of them gathered together to sing and to dance (Mosiah 20:1-2). The Bible clearly mentions the incident as a yearly ritual. The Book of Mormon mentions it as a regular occurrence, not telling us how often ("one day"). In both stories the kidnapped virgins became the wives of the abductors. The record says that the priests of Noah, "being ashamed to return to the city of Nephi, yea, and also fearing that the people would slay them, therefore they durst not return to their wives and their children" (Mosiah 20:3), so they watched the dancers and kidnapped substitute wives. When the narrative returned to the story of Amulon and his fellow priests, the daughters of the Lamanites were then called "their wives" (Mosiah 23:33). In both stories, the abductors, like peeping toms, waited and watched the spectacle. The Benjaminites lay in wait in the vineyards watching the dancing. The wicked priests also found the place where the girls danced, then "they laid and watched them" (Mosiah 20:4). We know that the priests hid because in the next verse they "came forth out of their secret places" and abducted twenty-four of the dancing maidens. Not only is the watching stressed in both stories, but also the lying in wait. These were not crimes of passion, but ones of premeditation.
quotes.attr: Alan Goff in John L. Sorenson and Melvin J. Thorne, eds., Rediscovering the Book of Mormon, p.68-69

notes: Number 24 This is the twenty-fourth F.A.R.M.S. monthly Update. It thus seems fitting to discuss the significance of the number 24 in ancient Israel and in the Book of Mormon. It is clear that certain numbers were meaningful in antiquity: 7 was the number of spiritual perfection (cf. 7 seals in the Book of Revelation); 12 was a governmental number (cf. 12 tribes, 12 apostles). The number 24, being a multiple of 12, was associated with heavenly government, especially priestly judgment and temple service. E. Bullinger, Number in Scripture (1894, reprinted Grand Rapids: Kregel Pub. 1969). At Qumran, judicial disputes were brought before a court called "the council of the community" (1QS 8:1). This deliberative body was composed of two panels of twelve, twelve priests and twelve laymen, for a quorum of 24 judges. These judges "give light by the judgment of the Urim and Thummim." Pesher on Isa. 54:11-12; J. Baumgarten, "The Duodecimal Courts of Qumran, Revelation, and the Sanhedrin," JBL 95 (1976), pp. 59-78. In the New Testament Apocalypse, 24 elders judge the world. These 24 elders are mentioned twelve times in the Book of Revelation (e.g., Rev. 4:4,10; 5:8,14; 14_3; 11:1p; 19:4). In Rabbinic Judaism, local courts having jurisdiction over most capital cases consisted of 24 (or 23) judges. These "Small Sanhedrins" were composed of two panels of twelve, one for the defense and the other for the prosecution (the odd number 23 prevented a tie vote and was a minimum quorum requirement). If one of the judges had to leave the trial, "it had to be ascertained if twenty-three. . . would be left, in which case he might go out; if not, he might not depart." Babylonian Talmud Sanhedrin 37a (the Mishnah lists decisions which require confirmation by the Urim and Thummim, Shebuoth 2:2). How far back can such duodecimal courts be found? The following evidence exists: Courts were established by Moses in each of the twelve tribes (Deut. 16:18). Jehoshaphat appointed "Levites, priests and elders" as judges (2 Chron. 19:8); related literature from Qumran assumes there were 12 in each group (IQM 2:1-3; Temple Sctoll). The apocalyptic idea of God being surrounded by a body of elders when he judges the world is at least as old as Isa. 24:23. Davidic tabernacle and Solomonic temple service were each continuously operated with 24 cour_es of priests (1 Chron. 24:3-18), and when David appointed his prophetic cantors, he established 24 orders each with 12 members (1 Chron. 25: 1-31). Thus, although we do not h direct evidence of duodecimal courts in pre-Exilic Israel, the indirect evidence along with the post-Exilic sources give that number presumptive significance in Lehi's day and before. Turning to the Book of Mormon, we see that 24 has remarkably similar significance: 1. It appears there were 24 judges on King Noah's court, since Noah and his priests kidnap 24 Lamanite daughters (Mos. 20:5). Alma's dismissal would have left 23 priests on the court. 2. There were 24 survivors of the final destruction of the Nephites who witnessed the judgment of God upon this people (Mor. 6:11, 15,22). There were other survivors (Mor. 6:15), but the 24 apparently stood as a body of special witnesses. 3. Particular mention is made of the number of the gold plates of Ether, probably because their number was 24 (Mos. 8:9; Alma 37:21; Eth. 1:2). These plates were seen as a record the "judgments of God" upon those people (Alma 37:30). Their contents were brought "to light" by the use of two seer stones (Mos. 28:13-16; Alma 37:21-25). 4. God's heavenly court which passed judgment upon Jerusalem (1 Ne. 1:13) consisted of 12 members (1: 10) . 5. Like the 12 apostles, the 12 Nephite disciples for a total of 24) will act as final judges (3 Ne. 27:27). 6. Perhaps it is not coincidence that Mormon, the "idle witness" (Mor. 3:16), was given charge of the legal records at age 24 (1:3), and that Helaman I and II were about that age when they were given the records too. 7. We can also note that the Jaredite king Orihah, the root of whose name may be related to "light" (Hebrew 'or, and 'urim), and whose single recorded virtue was that he "executed days" (Eth. 7:1), had 23 sons perhaps sitting judgment upon the land in righteousness all his with him as judges (7:2; cf. 1

“Bride capture is, in fact, an old idea and was found throughout much of the ancient world, not just in Israel. The fact that two different Israelite groups practiced it on a one-time basis is not at all unexpected, particularly if the priests of Noah were acquainted with the story from Judges 19-21.”

quotes.text: Chron. 25:1). 8. The text of the governmental oath of the Nephite chief judge to "judge righteouslv" happens to be reported only in the account of the twenty-fourth year of the reign of Jud (Alma 50:39-40). Prophets, judges, witnesses, God's tribunal, God's judgment, the Urim and Thummin, heavenly righteous government are all elements of what Baumgarten calls "duodecimal symbolism." (p. 65). These elements appear with remarkable consistency associated with the number 24 in the texts of the Book of Mormon.

notes: lion (Heb. labiy') to roar, the lion is also a symbol of power
dragons (Heb. tanniyn) sea monster; serpent, venomous snake
Behold, here is the king of the Lamanites; he having received a wound has fallen among their dead, and they have left him; and behold, we have brought him before you; and now let us slay him.

Verse 19: "And now, behold, and tell the king of these things, that he may tell his people that they may be pacified towards us; for behold they are already preparing to come against us; and behold also there are but few of us [we are in a bad fix]. And behold, they come with their numerous hosts; and except the king doth pacify them towards us we must perish. For are not the words of Abinadi fulfilled, which he prophesied against us [now this is the old fire-eater Gideon speaking; he's a great patriot, but he says, it's our blame-we are responsible. `We are their author and original,' as Titania says]-and all this because we would not hearken unto the words of the Lord, and turn from our iniquities? And now let us pacify the king, and we fulfil the oath which we have made unto him." Notice that he is being very realistic; these are the steps by which the problem is solved here. It's a very touchy thing, but they solve

quotes.text: Notice that he is being very realistic; these are the steps by which the problem is solved here. It's a very touchy thing, but they solve the problem very sensibly on both sides-a thing people rarely do…Gideon is the last man you would expect to do this. But he had the experience of these things, and he knew. It's the old commander that knows. The most passionate talks I've ever heard against war in the Army have been by generals, without any exception. They know what it is, and boy do they light in! There were some wonderful ones by Omar Bradley, Max Taylor, and others. Eisenhower said some pretty strong things too. ‘…therefore, let us put a stop to the shedding of so much blood,’ Gideon said, with his rush of excitement. This is the Gideon who chased the king up the tower with a sword, and all that sort of thing. He is the one who is making a plea to put an end to all this bloodshed, whatever they do.
quotes.speaker: Hugh Nibley
quotes.source: Teachings of the Book of Mormon
quotes.attr: Hugh Nibley, Teachings of the Book of Mormon, Lecture 38, p. 138

notes: Limhi's people are smitten and defeated by the Lamanites—They meet Ammon and are converted—They tell Ammon of the twenty-four Jaredite plates. About 122–121 B.C.
